stem: A curve is given by \( x=[[a]]t^{2} \), \( y=[[b]]t^{3} \). Find \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) at \( t=[[t0]] \).
A curve is given by \( x=[[a]]\cos t \), \( y=[[b]]\sin t \). Find \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) at \( t=\dfrac{\pi}{[[n]]} \).
solution: \( \frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}=\frac{[[b]]\cos t}{-[[a]]\sin t}=-\frac{[[b]]}{[[a]]}\cot t \); at \( t=\pi/[[n]] \) this is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the required minus sign from the quotient rule on \( dx/dt=-a\sin t\).
This inverts the ratio \( b/a\).
This uses \( \tan t\) instead of \( \cot t\).
This forgets to evaluate the trig factor at the given angle.
This is off by a small additive amount from the correct slope.
This is off by a small additive amount (other direction) from the correct slope.

stem: A particle moves in the plane so that \( \dfrac{dx}{dt}=[[a]]t+[[b]] \) and \( \dfrac{dy}{dt}=[[c]]t+[[d]] \) for all \( t \). Find \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) at \( t=[[t0]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, using \( dx/dt\) over \( dy/dt\).
This drops a sign in the numerator.
This drops a sign in the denominator.
This subtracts the two velocity components instead of dividing them.
This is off by one from the correct slope.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct slope.

stem: A curve is given by \( x=e^{[[a]]t} \), \( y=e^{[[b]]t} \). Find \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) at \( t=[[t0]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the coefficient ratio \( b/a\).
This flips the sign of the exponent.
This forgets to include the exponential factor entirely.
This drops the coefficient ratio, leaving only the exponential factor.
This is off by one from the correct slope.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct slope.

stem: A curve is given by \( x=[[a]]t^{2} \), \( y=[[b]]t^{3} \). Find the \( y \)-intercept of the line tangent to the curve at \( t=[[t0]] \).
solution: The point is \( ([[a]]\cdot[[t0]]^{2},[[b]]\cdot[[t0]]^{3}) \) and the slope is \( \frac{3\cdot[[b]]\cdot[[t0]]}{2\cdot[[a]]} \); the tangent line is \( y-[[b]]\cdot[[t0]]^{3}=\frac{3\cdot[[b]]\cdot[[t0]]}{2\cdot[[a]]}(x-[[a]]\cdot[[t0]]^{2}) \), which has \( y \)-intercept \( -\frac{1}{2}[[b]]\cdot[[t0]]^{3}=[[answer]] \) (the leading coefficient \( a\) cancels out).
distractor_rationales: This drops the required minus sign.
This forgets the factor of \( \tfrac12\).
This uses \( t_0^2\) instead of \( t_0^3\).
This uses a factor of \( \tfrac32\) instead of \( \tfrac12\).
This is off by one from the correct \( y\)-intercept.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct \( y\)-intercept.

stem: A curve is given by \( x=t \), \( y=\dfrac{[[c]]}{t} \). Find \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) at \( t=[[t0]] \).
solution: Since \( y=[[c]]/t \), \( \dfrac{dy}{dt}=-\dfrac{[[c]]}{t^{2}} \) and \( \dfrac{dx}{dt}=1 \), so \( \dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{[[c]]}{t^{2}} \); at \( t=[[t0]] \) this is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the required minus sign from the power rule on a negative exponent.
This forgets to square \( t\) in the denominator.
This inverts the fraction entirely.
This has the wrong sign and forgets the power rule's exponent drop.
This is off by one from the correct slope.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct slope.

stem: A curve is given by \( x=\ln t \), \( y=[[a]]t^{2}+[[b]] \), for \( t>0 \). Find \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} \) at \( t=[[t0]] \).
solution: \( \dfrac{dy}{dt}=2\cdot[[a]]t \) and \( \dfrac{dx}{dt}=\dfrac{1}{t} \), so \( \dfrac{dy}{dx}=2\cdot[[a]]t\cdot t=2\cdot[[a]]t^{2} \); at \( t=[[t0]] \) this is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to double the coefficient \( a\).
This forgets to square \( t\), leaving it linear.
This divides by \( t^2\) instead of multiplying.
This mistakenly includes the constant \( b\), which vanishes under differentiation.
This is off by one from the correct slope.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct slope.
